steps.title: Identify the core claim to weaken
steps.content: The traditional view claims “voting in an election does not change a voter's attitude.” To weaken this claim, we need evidence showing the opposite—that voting DOES change attitudes in some measurable way.

steps.title: Match evidence showing voting's effect
steps.content:
Voters held significantly more polarized (extreme) attitudes than non-voters two years after the election, showing that voting itself affected how attitudes developed relative to not voting—which directly contradicts the claim that voting doesn't change attitudes.

The other options either suggest attitudes are driven by general political orientation regardless of voting, demonstrate attitudes change even without voting, or show both groups experienced similar attitude shifts.

This confirms C is correct.
